museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: Atari's Pac-Man reaches the 2600, Robotron debuts at the operators' show, and the gap between arcade dream and home cartridge reality becomes visible.
museum.curatorNote: March 1982 is anchored by Atari 2600 Pac-Man and Robotron's March AOE debut. Exact retail availability can vary by source, so dates are presented carefully.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: March tests the home arcade promise.

items.title: Pac-Man reaches Atari 2600 shelves
items.body: Atari's home Pac-Man arrived amid huge expectation. It sold enormously, but also exposed the limits of translating an arcade phenomenon to a tiny cartridge.
items.sourceNote: MobyGames lists March 16, 1982; other sources cite March 1982 with April 3 Pac-Man Day promotion.

items.title: Robotron: 2084 debuts at AOE
items.body: Williams showed Robotron at the Amusement Operators Expo, presenting a brutally fast twin-stick vision of arcade intensity.
items.sourceNote: Robotron references state Williams debuted the game at the Amusement Operators Expo in March 1982.

items.title: Pac-Man Day approaches
items.body: Atari's promotional push around home Pac-Man treated a cartridge like a public event, culminating in April's National Pac-Man Day.
items.sourceNote: AtariProtos and Pac-Man references describe April 3, 1982 Pac-Man Day after March retail availability.

items.title: UK Spectrum anticipation enters final stretch
items.body: The ZX Spectrum would launch in April, but March magazines and adverts already made the cheap colour micro feel imminent.
items.sourceNote: ZX Spectrum launch followed on 23 April 1982.

items.title: Arcade controls get more specialised
items.body: Robotron's dual-stick layout underlined how arcade cabinets could be custom machines, not just screens with joysticks.
items.sourceNote: Robotron control scheme and March debut context.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: Eight March-window items, with arcade and home-console tension at the centre.

items.title: Pac-Man
items.body: The year's most infamous home arcade translation begins its commercial life.
items.sourceNote: MobyGames/GameFAQs list March 1982; April 3 was promotional Pac-Man Day.

items.title: Robotron: 2084
items.body: Shown in March; a twin-stick arcade object built around panic and control separation.
items.sourceNote: AOE debut in March; wider release timing described as 1982.

items.title: Dig Dug
items.body: Fresh from February Japan, soon spreading internationally in April.

items.title: Ms. Pac-Man
items.body: Already becoming the arcade sequel that felt like a full personality rather than a quick variant.

items.title: Zaxxon
items.body: Its visual trick remained one of early 1982's strongest cabinet memories.

items.title: BBC Micro games and listings
items.body: The BBC Micro's serious reputation did not prevent it from becoming a games machine in practice.

items.title: ZX81 cassette software
items.body: Before the Spectrum, low-cost Sinclair software already trained UK players in tape patience.

items.title: Arcade conversions in magazines
items.body: The phrase arcade conversion carried both hope and danger in a month defined by Pac-Man.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: March is where hardware limitations become visible.

items.title: Atari 2600 memory limits
items.body: Pac-Man made millions of players think about the difference between the arcade board and the home cartridge, even if they did not name the chips.

items.title: Robotron control panel
items.body: Two joysticks made the cabinet feel like a machine built for one idea only.

items.title: UK television as monitor
items.body: Home micros and consoles made the family TV part of gaming hardware.

items.title: Cassette deck dependence
items.body: For computer games, the tape recorder was becoming as important as the keyboard.

sections.title: Magazine Covers
sections.subtitle: The March shelf explained the difference between cabinet and cartridge.

items.title: Pac-Man at home
items.headline: Arcade hit becomes home cartridge
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed games magazine cover for March 1982.
items.curatorNote: Pac-Man made the home conversion a mainstream magazine question.
items.body: Pac-Man made the home conversion a mainstream magazine question.
items.sourceNote: Specific March cover line not asserted.

items.title: C&VG arcade/home comparison
items.headline: Coin-op glamour, home compromises
items.curatorNote: The UK press had to serve arcade watchers and home-computer owners at the same time.
items.body: The UK press had to serve arcade watchers and home-computer owners at the same time.

items.title: Microcomputer listings
items.headline: Keyboard machines as game platforms
items.curatorNote: Serious computing magazines were accidentally building the games audience too.
items.body: Serious computing magazines were accidentally building the games audience too.

items.title: Weekly micro adverts
items.headline: The near-live micro feed
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed Popular Computing Weekly cover for March 1982.
items.curatorNote: Weekly pages made machines feel like an unfolding market rather than fixed products.
items.body: Weekly pages made machines feel like an unfolding market rather than fixed products.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: The network was still social and printed.

items.title: Cabinet watching was discovery
items.body: Players learned Robotron by watching hands as much as screens.

items.title: Magazine criticism mattered
items.body: A printed review could make sense of why home Pac-Man felt wrong.

items.title: Typed listings were shared labour
items.body: A game could arrive as text and become software only through effort.

items.title: BBS culture was niche
items.body: Networked computing existed, but everyday game discovery was still mostly offline.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: March is the month the arcade dream hit the living-room wall.

items.title: The box could disappoint
items.body: Pac-Man proved that owning the name was not the same as owning the arcade.

items.title: Control mattered
items.body: Robotron showed that the feel of a cabinet could be inseparable from its hardware.

items.title: The TV became contested space
items.body: Games needed the family screen, which meant negotiation.

items.title: The Spectrum was almost here
items.body: UK players could feel the price of entry about to change.

sources.label: Pac-Man Atari 2600 release context
sources.note: Used for March 1982 release and April 3 promotion context.

sources.label: Pac-Man Atari 2600 MobyGames release
sources.note: Used for March 16, 1982 listing while keeping month-level phrasing.

sources.label: Robotron 2084 context
sources.note: Used for March 1982 AOE debut and control context.

sources.label: ZX Spectrum launch
sources.note: Used for April 23 launch and March build-up.
